_comment: Firestore seed data. Top-level keys map to collection names. Import via Firebase CLI or Admin SDK.

luhya.name: Luhya
luhya.overview: The Luhya — also written Luyia or Abaluyia — are the second-largest ethnic group in Kenya, numbering approximately 7 million people. They occupy the fertile lands of western Kenya, primarily in the counties of Kakamega, Bungoma, Vihiga, Trans Nzoia, and parts of Busia. The name Abaluyia means 'those of the same hearth' — a reference to the shared fireplace that once symbolised community belonging. The Luhya are not a single tribe but a confederation of 18 sub-groups, each with distinct dialects, traditions, and historical identities, united by a shared Bantu origin, cultural practices, and geographic proximity.
luhya.language_notes: Oluhya is not a single language but a dialect continuum — a family of related Bantu dialects that are mutually intelligible to varying degrees. Lubukusu (spoken by the Bukusu) and Luragoli (spoken by the Maragoli) are the most divergent. Most Luhya speak both their sub-group dialect and a regional creole form of Oluhya, alongside Swahili and English.

key_phrases.meaning: a child is the child of everyone

origin_stories.title: Mwambu and Sela — the first Luhya man and woman
origin_stories.body:
In the beginning, Were — the supreme creator — made Mwambu, the first man, and placed him on earth. He gave him Sela for a wife. Mwambu and Sela were the ancestors of the Abaluyia. Were told them: 'The land is yours, tend it well. The cattle are yours, care for them. Your children shall fill the earth.'

Mwambu and Sela had many children, and as the family grew they spread across the western highlands. Each child became the founder of a sub-group — Bukusu, Maragoli, Wanga — each carrying a part of the original instruction from Were. The Luhya say: Omwana ka omukana — a child is the child of everyone.
origin_stories.attribution: Oral tradition · documented in Oluhya and English · Western Kenya

origin_stories.title: Maina wa Mutsembi and the Bukusu circumcision rite
origin_stories.body:
The Bukusu circumcision — Imbalu — is performed every even-numbered year and is among the most significant cultural events in western Kenya. Young men are circumcised publicly without anaesthetic as a test of courage and entry into adulthood.

The ceremony begins with the candidate smearing white clay on their body and dancing through the village before dawn. Elders, family, and community gather to witness. A man who flinches is considered to have shamed his lineage. The scar is permanent — it is the mark of having stood.
origin_stories.attribution: Documented by Luhya Cultural Council · Bungoma County

food_traditions.name: Ugali
food_traditions.description: Maize or sorghum flour ugali — the daily staple, served with vegetables or stew.

food_traditions.name: Isombe
food_traditions.description: Pounded cassava leaves cooked with groundnuts. A Luhya staple particularly associated with Kakamega.

food_traditions.name: Ingokho
food_traditions.description: Local free-range chicken, central to celebrations and hospitality. A household that serves ingokho honours its guests.

food_traditions.name: Kunde
food_traditions.description: Cowpea leaves — a nutritious green vegetable cooked with onions and tomatoes.

food_traditions.name: Mrenda
food_traditions.description: A mucilaginous leafy vegetable (jute mallow) popular in western Kenya.

food_traditions.name: Ekeberi
food_traditions.description: Cow innards prepared as a delicacy, typically during celebrations.

music_traditions.name: Imbalu circumcision chant
music_traditions.description: Pre-dawn ceremony song performed before the circumcision rite begins.

music_traditions.name: Isukuti drum ensemble
music_traditions.description: Traditional celebration drumming performed at weddings, funerals, and harvest festivals. The isukuti is a large double-headed drum.

music_traditions.name: Omwana alilira
music_traditions.description: A lullaby sung by mothers during the harvest season.

notable_practices.name: Ceremonial bull slaughter
notable_practices.description: A household that can slaughter a bull for a guest demonstrates wealth and honour. The act of sharing food from a common pot (esindu) is itself a statement of kinship.

notable_practices.name: Were — the supreme being
notable_practices.description: The Luhya supreme creator, Were, is acknowledged in daily speech and invoked during important life events. The relationship between humans and Were shapes Luhya moral cosmology.
